# `node_modules/` at repo root

> **What is this folder?**  
> It holds **dependencies downloaded by npm** — thousands of small packages your tools need to run. You did not write this code; maintainers publish it to the registry and npm copies it here.

---

## HarmonyForge: this is not your “main” install

This repo runs as **two separate Node projects**:

| Where | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| **`frontend/node_modules/`** | Next.js app, RiffScore, tests |
| **`backend/node_modules/`** | Engine, Express API, Jest |

There is **no `package.json` at the repo root**, so a **`node_modules/` here is unusual**. It often appears after an accidental `npm install` in the wrong directory, an old workflow, or a global tool hoisting packages.

**For day-to-day work, use:**

```bash
# from repo root
make install
```

That installs into **`backend/`** and **`frontend/`**, where the real apps live.

---

## Can I delete this folder?

| Situation | Safe? |
|-----------|--------|
| You only use `make install` / `npm install` inside **`frontend/`** and **`backend/`** | **Yes** — you can remove **root** `node_modules` to save disk space. |
| Something you run **from the repo root** breaks without it | Investigate that script; prefer moving installs into **`frontend/`** or **`backend/`**. |

Deleting **`frontend/node_modules`** or **`backend/node_modules`** means you must run **`npm install`** again in those folders (or `make install`).

---

## Should this be in Git?

**No.** Dependencies are reproducible from each **`package.json`** + lockfile. Committing `node_modules` bloats the repo and causes merge pain.

Only **this `README.md`** is meant to be tracked (see root **`.gitignore`**), as a signpost for anyone who lands in this folder.

---

## What’s inside (conceptually)

```text
node_modules/
├── README.md          ← you are here (HarmonyForge note)
├── <package-a>/       ← one npm package per folder
├── <package-b>/
└── …                  ← many more; do not edit by hand
```

If you open a subfolder, you’ll see that package’s `package.json`, license, and compiled code — useful for debugging, not for project documentation.
